So in the software world, where people build applications and things like that, there's this concept called an MVP and it stands for minimum viable product. And basically what happened in the software world is a lot of these software applications kept getting built and people started just adding tons of features into these things. Like, they were making them more complicated, more complicated, more complicated, more complicated and the industry kind of turned into a place where people would just add as many features as possible. That was how they thought that they made their product better than someone else's. And it ended up kind of like the shopping bag mentality. Most software companies thought of their application as a shopping bag and they figured the more stuff they put in the bag, the better. So they would build tons and tons and tons of different features and they'd end up with these massive, complicated, rocket spaceship sort of software applications. And then what happened is people were like, man, this software is just so confusing. It's got so many buttons on it. It's so complicated. All I need to do is just send an email. And so then what started happening is a few people, a few disruptive people came up with the idea called the minimum viable product. And that was the minimum amount of features so that you could actually just satisfy the customer, just satisfy, just cover his needs. And so what happened is if someone needed an application just to send emails, they would build a minimum viable product, which would mean it could only send emails. They didn't add any of these other features in there. They just left it bare bones. And what started happening is these companies started disrupting the companies that made very complicated software products. And then in the end, a lot of the market was like, well, I'd rather a simple program or a simple software application than a big, complicated one. So the market started turning towards minimum viable products instead of going with these big, fancy, complicated software programs. So there's three cardinal rules of a minimum viable offer. Rule number one is talk about the transformation and the results, not about what you'll do for them. So a lot of people, when they offer people their services, they say, oh, I have this very unique process and basically what it involves is I'm going to do 100 hours of this. I'm going to come to your office. I'm going to be sweating. I'm going to hook up all of these computers into your system. I'm going to be doing so much work that you're going to be blown away. And no one wants that. If you, truthfully, if I hire someone to do something and they tell me that they're going to be rummaging around my office for days and sweating and doing all this stuff, I don't want that. That's the last thing I want. I'd prefer not to even meet the person. I'd just prefer for them to fix it and be done. And so we shouldn't talk about what we're going to do for them. We should only talk about the transformation and the results. So we should talk about taking them from their current situation to their desired situation and what life is going to be like when we do that. Rule number two is pricing on value. So we want to price on the value of having this transformation, not the hard costs or time involved with you delivering it. So the classical school of thought, the classical way of thinking is that we should charge per hour and then the cost of our services will just be determined by how many hours we do. Well, that's silly because that's really just encouraging the consultant to spend as much hours as possible on it, which isn't good for you because, and it's not like he's going to be, he's going to want to try and get it done real quick because he's getting paid per hour. And so there's a silly way of thinking. There's a totally mad way of pricing things. And you shouldn't price on how much it costs you. You shouldn't calculate how much the gas, how much gas it costs you to drive out to the office. You shouldn't calculate the air fees or how much paper you used or how much a staple costs for the pen and the pen that you were writing with. You don't price things that way either. You price on value. So what is it worth for someone to have this problem solved? If someone's got a company and it's losing $10,000 every single month, well, what's it worth for them to fix that? Now, what is the value of them achieving this transformation? So if they fix this problem, what is it worth to them? And now you want to price your program between $2,500 and $10,000, maybe even more. Again, you want to price on value. Now, typically if you're doing coaching or any sort of program, I don't think you should be charging less than $2,500. You want to be solving a problem which is painful enough. You want to be making a very, very profound transformation for someone. There's no point getting into consulting and coaching and all other stuff just to help someone make an incremental improvement. You want to choose and tackle a big, nasty problem. And you want to help someone make a profound, life-changing, earth-shaking transformation. Because the bigger the transformation, the nastier the problem, the more money someone is willing to pay. And the more eager they're going to be to sign up for your services and all of that stuff. Another one is forming a niche offer result hypothesis and confusing constants for variables and making a big mess. Let me explain this in a bit more detail. So let's get this straight right now. You can only have one variable at a time. So in a scientific experiment, you have a constant and a variable and a result. You know, like I'll give you very basic stuff here. Let's say we want to measure the boiling temperature of water with different, we want to measure the, you know, how long it takes for water to boil with a Bunsen burner based on the different Bunsen burners. Well, if we're going to do that experiment, we need to keep the water the same. We want to have the same volume of water and we want to have the same beaker every single time. That has to be kept constant. And then our variable is the thing which we're testing, which is different Bunsen burners. If we go out and we change the water every time, we put, you know, a massive jug of water in one time, tiny amount of water in the next time, some salt water in the next time, and we're changing these Bunsen burners at the same time, our experiment's going to be a big mess. We're not going to know anything. We have to hold a constant and then we have to have a variable. And it's the same in business. Because we use a scientific method for everything we do with consulting. Because this process isn't about chance. This process is precise science. So here's how it works for us. We've got a constant, a variable, and a constant. So what we know is our niche, because we pick our niche, and we know who they are. So that's a constant. So desired situation result, the niche is the niche. We've got both sides of this equation here. We've got the constant and we've got the other constant. Now we're only going to be changing and playing with one thing here, which is the variable. And that is our offer. So we come up with an offer and we test it. So we keep adjusting the offer and we keep going back to the market and testing. So over a few tests, we're gonna figure out what the right variable is.
